mainEntity.name: How much does an XK engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: An XK engine replacement costs between £3,000 and £10,000 supply-only depending on engine choice — the 4.0 AJ27 sits at the lower end (£3,000–£5,500), while the 5.0 AJ133 supercharged sits at the top (£6,500–£10,000) [JAG-QUOTE]. Labour adds approximately 12–18 hours at £65–£95/hr for an independent specialist. Use our diagnostic calculator to get a personalised estimate for your specific XK.

mainEntity.name: Which XK engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The AJ133 5.0 V8 (2009–2014) is the most reliable XK engine. The 2009 facelift brought a revised timing chain tensioner design that resolved the weakness of earlier AJ-V8s, and the 5.0-litre unit has proven robust across the X150 range. The naturally aspirated 385bhp version is the sweet spot — simpler than the supercharged variant with fewer potential failure points.

mainEntity.name: Which XK engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: Avoid the early AJ27 4.0 V8 (1996–2000) without documented history — these engines suffer from bore wear (blue smoke on throttle, misfires) and timing chain tensioner issues. If you're set on an X100, buy a 2003+ 4.2 car — the AJ34 is significantly more robust. For the X150, all engines are solid, but prioritise a 2009+ 5.0 car for the best reliability.

mainEntity.name: Does the XK have timing chain problems?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — pre-2009 AJ-V8 engines (AJ27, early AJ34) have a documented timing chain tensioner weakness. Cold-start rattle is the early warning sign. If you hear it, book an inspection immediately — ignoring it can cause catastrophic engine failure. Post-2009 5.0 AJ133 engines have a revised design and are much more robust.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an XK engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on the car's value. A 2014 XKR-S worth £30,000–£35,000 with a £6,500–£10,000 recon cost sits at 20–30% of vehicle value — absolutely worth replacing. A 1998 XK8 worth £4,000–£5,000 facing a £5,000+ recon cost sits at over 100% of vehicle value — not worth it. If replacement cost exceeds roughly 60% of your car's market value, get a scrap valuation before committing. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Which XK should I buy — X100 or X150?

acceptedAnswer.text: Buy an X150 (2009–2014) with the 5.0 naturally aspirated V8 — it's the best-looking generation, the most reliable engine, and values are still £10,000–£20,000, which is remarkably low for a car of this quality. The X100 is a beautiful classic but the economics are tighter — only buy one if you're prepared for classic-car ownership costs.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my XK engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Your XK's engine code can be found in three places: (1) on the VIN plate inside the driver's door sill — the engine code is part of the VIN; (2) on the engine block itself — stamped on the front of the AJ-V8 block; (3) in your service documentation or V5C logbook. Our 15+ vetted Jaguar specialists can also identify your engine if you're unsure.

mainEntity.name: XK vs XKR — which is more reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both use the same AJ-V8 engine family — the XKR simply adds a supercharger. The naturally aspirated XK has fewer components to fail (no supercharger, no intercooler), so it's technically the more reliable option. However, the XKR's supercharged V8 is robust when maintained — 2009+ examples with the 5.0 AJ133S have proven very reliable. Our honest advice: buy the XKR if you want the performance; the reliability difference is minor. Read the full comparison: XK vs XKR → /compare/xk-vs-xkr/

hero.h1: XK Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Two generations of Jaguar's V8-powered grand tourer — X100 and X150 — covered from the 4.0 AJ27 to the 5.0 AJ133 supercharged. Real UK failure patterns, honest replacement economics, and costs you can actually trust. Backed by 290+ real UK XK engine enquiries in 2025 [JAG-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The XK rule: generation and engine choice determine whether replacement makes financial sense — an early 4.0 XK8 can fail the threshold, a 5.0 XKR-S comfortably passes it
AJ133 5.0 V8 — revised timing chain tensioner design from 2009 onward, robust supercharged V8
AJ27 4.0 V8 — bore wear and timing chain tensioner issues on early X100 cars [PATTERN DATA]
X150 (2009–2014) 5.0 naturally aspirated — facelift car, revised engine, strong value retention
💎 Premium Style — the grand tourer that defines Jaguar elegance

generations.subHeadline: Two generations of V8-powered grand touring — the steel-bodied X100 and the aluminium-monocoque X150.

cards.title: X100 (1996–2006) —
cards.verdict: The original modern XK — beautiful, characterful, and increasingly collectable. Buy a 2003+ 4.2 car with full history; early 4.0 cars need bore-wear checks and timing chain inspection before purchase.

cards.title: X150 (2006–2014)
cards.verdict: The definitive XK — Ian Callum's aluminium masterpiece. The 2009 facelift brought the 5.0 V8 with much-improved timing chain design. This is the one to buy, especially the naturally aspirated 5.0 Portfolio or the thunderous XKR-S.

cards.title: The 2009 Facelift — The Dividing Line
cards.meta: Pre-2009 (4.2-litre): The original X150 launched in 2006 with the 4.2 AJ34 V8 — a proven unit, but the 5.0 that replaced it is objectively better in every way.

comparisonLink.label: Read the full comparison: X100 vs X150 →

engineDatabase.h2: XK Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every V8 that powered the XK — verified factory codes, real UK enquiry data, and honest reliability ratings.

commonProblems.subHeadline: What actually fails on the XK's AJ-V8 engines — and what it costs to fix.

problems.description: Early X100 engines (pre-2000) can suffer cylinder bore wear, causing misfires and blue exhaust smoke on throttle. Full engine rebuild is the only permanent fix.
Pre-2009 AJ27 and early AJ34 engines have a documented timing chain tensioner weakness. Cold-start rattle is the early warning sign — catastrophic if ignored.
The 5.0 V8's water pump is a known weak point across the AJ133 family. Owner reports confirm it's a routine replacement item — catch it before overheating causes head-gasket damage.
Shared across the X150 range — torque converter lock-up clutch failure causes gearbox slippage, grinding noises and transmission warning lights.
XKs are sensitive to battery condition — modules failing to sleep correctly is a frequent no-start complaint. Always use a premium battery.
Faulty injectors on the 5.0 AJ133 cause rough running, reduced power, stalling and excessive fuel consumption.

marketIntelligence.h2: XK Market Intelligence — What 290+ UK Owners Told Us in 2025
insights: Interesting insight: The X150 generation accounts for roughly 60% of all XK enquiries in 2025 — the aluminium grand tourer is the one buyers are actively researching, while the X100 is increasingly the preserve of collectors and enthusiasts

The 5.0 AJ133 engine (fitted to 2009–2014 X150s) generates significantly more enquiry volume than any other XK engine — buyers are actively seeking the post-facelift car, and specialists report this is the generation with the strongest reliability record

Early 4.0 X100 cars generate relatively low enquiry volume but high forum activity — the enthusiast community is active, but the cars are now cheap enough that a full engine replacement often fails the economics threshold for all but the best examples

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every XK Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: Buy a 2009–2014 X150 with the 5.0 naturally aspirated V8, full service history, and evidence the water pump has been replaced. It's the sweet spot — the most reliable engine, the best-looking generation, and values that are still £10,000–£20,000 below where they should be.

replacementCosts.h2: XK Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for every XK V8 — used, reconditioned and rebuilt, supply-only, with independent labour guidance.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting an XK engine typically takes 12–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [JAG-QUOTE]. The XK rule — the value swing is the story: The XK's value range is one of the widest in the Jaguar line-up. An early X100 XK8 worth £4,000–£5,000 facing a £5,000+ reconditioned engine sits at 100%+ of vehicle value — well above our 60% scrap threshold for Jaguar. The same engine in a 2014 XKR-S worth £30,000–£35,000 sits at just 20–30% of vehicle value — a no-brainer replacement. Before committing to a recon engine, get a realistic market valuation for your specific car and run the numbers. For X100s, used engines often make more sense; for X150s, a reconditioned unit is usually the right call.

engineEvolution.h2: How the XK Engine Range Evolved — 1996 to 2014

eras.whyBmwChanged: The original XK8 launched with Jaguar's new 4.0-litre V8 — a clean-sheet design replacing the straight-six XJS.
eras.worthKnowing: Early cars (pre-2000) have a reputation for bore wear — always inspect before buying.

eras.whyBmwChanged: Jaguar increased displacement to 4.2 litres in 2002, bringing more power and improved reliability — the bore-wear issues were largely resolved.
eras.worthKnowing: The 4.2 is significantly more robust than the early 4.0 — this is the X100 to target.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The X150 launched with an aluminium monocoque, losing 150kg over the X100 — the 4.2 engine carried over initially.
eras.worthKnowing: The X150 chassis is a masterpiece — Ian Callum's finest work.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The 2009 facelift brought the 5.0-litre AJ133 V8 with 385bhp (naturally aspirated) and up to 542bhp (supercharged) — plus a revised timing chain tensioner design.
eras.worthKnowing: This is the definitive XK engine — more power, stronger reliability, and the one specialists recommend.

profiles.verdict: The XKR-S is one of the most underrated performance cars on the used market — 542bhp, 200mph+, and values that haven't yet caught up to the driving experience. Buy a 2012+ car with full service history.

The XK is a genuine GT — comfortable, refined, and capable of daily use. But be realistic: fuel economy is 18–25mpg, and maintenance costs reflect the original £70,000 price tag. A 2009+ 5.0 naturally aspirated is the sweet spot.
The XK is a 2+2 at best — rear seats are tiny, and even children will complain. This is a car for couples, not families. If you need practicality, look at the F-Pace instead.
An early X100 can be bought for under £5,000 — but a £5,000 engine replacement will quickly exceed the car's value. Buy the best X150 5.0 you can afford, not the cheapest X100 you can find.
The XK, especially the X150, is a future classic. Low-mileage examples with full service history are already appreciating. The XKR-S and Portfolio models are the ones to hold long-term.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My XK Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: The XK presents one of the widest economics swings in the Jaguar range — a £5,000 reconditioned engine in a £4,000 X100 is a financial mistake, but the same engine in a £30,000 XKR-S is a no-brainer. Run the numbers before you commit.

signals.title: Built on Real Data
signals.text: Every reliability rating and cost range in this XK guide comes from genuine UK quote and enquiry data — 290+ real XK enquiries in 2025 [JAG-VERIFIED] — not manufacturer marketing, not forum guesswork.

signals.title: We Tell You When Not to Repair
signals.text: The XK rule is clear: if reconditioned engine cost exceeds roughly 60% of what your specific car is worth, get a scrap valuation alongside any repair quote. We tell XK owners when replacement doesn't make financial sense.

signals.title: Vetted Jaguar Specialists
signals.text: Our network includes 15+ vetted Jaguar specialists who work on XK engines daily — from AJ27 bore-wear diagnosis to AJ133 water pump replacement. No generalists — only Jaguar-specific expertise.

signals.title: Part of Engine Finders
signals.text: JaguarEngine.uk is part of the Engine Finders family, the UK's leading engine replacement marketplace — giving you the research depth of an independent authority with a vetted route to action when you need one.
